summary: Patrick McKenzie shares a mid-month financial update for Bingo Card Creator, reporting $332 in net sales and $290 in profit — a dramatic improvement over his previous months of $25 and -$2. He tracks a promising sales progression of 1, 2, 6, 8, 16 sales per two-week interval since launch, while cautioning against assuming geometric growth will continue. He outlines plans to reach $600 in monthly sales through Mac distribution, Halloween-themed SEO content, new advertising campaigns, and a CD version of the product. The post closes with a personal note about using the profits to pay off his student loan early and buy a Nintendo Wii.
key_points: Net sales reached $332 mid-month with only $42 in expenses, yielding nearly $290 in profit — orders of magnitude better than prior months
Sales have followed a roughly doubling pattern (1, 2, 6, 8, 16 per two-week interval) but he warns against extrapolating geometric growth
His growth strategy is multi-pronged: Mac distribution, seasonal SEO content (Halloween bingo), new ad campaigns, CD product launch, and purchase page optimization
Total business expenses remain remarkably low at under $50/month between hosting and AdWords
money_quotes: Well, that's certainly a sliiiiiight increase from my last two months ($25 and -$2, as I recall).
Unfortunately, I think the 'it will continue increasing geometrically!' assumption you might have from high school algebra is a tad aggressive.
I think I'm well below the maximum amount of sales I can get with reasonable effort.
I've got more promotions to try, more advertising campaigns to run, more tricks up my sleeve, more products to launch.
I'm getting my Halloween bingo page ready this weekend so it gets search engine rankings in advance of the actual day.
key_insight: Early-stage software businesses can achieve meaningful profit growth through disciplined, incremental marketing efforts even when starting from nearly zero revenue.
